section.title: Prayer for the Ephesians
section.sectionRationale: Resuming the sentence he broke off in 3:1, Paul kneels before the Father and prays for the church he has just described as one new humanity and a dwelling place for God. His petition matches his theology: that the God whose fullness fills the temple would strengthen these believers by the Spirit, so that Christ dwells in their hearts and they grasp the immeasurable love of Christ and are filled with all the fullness of God. The section closes chapters one to three with a soaring doxology to the God who does far more than we can ask or imagine.
section.thesis: Paul bows before the Father and asks that out of His glorious riches He would strengthen the Ephesians by the Spirit in their inner being, so that Christ dwells in their hearts by faith and they, rooted in love, come with all the saints to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge and are filled with all the fullness of God, closing in praise to the God who can do immeasurably more than we ask or imagine.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Paul kneels before the Father from whom every family is named; the petition for inner strength, Christ's indwelling, and being rooted in love; the prayer to know the surpassing love of Christ and be filled with God's fullness; and the closing doxology to the God who does immeasurably more.

units.label: Paul kneels before the Father
units.note: from whom every family is named

verses.text: ... for this reason I bow my knees before the Father,
from whom every {{patria|family}} in heaven and on earth derives its name.

units.label: Strengthened, indwelt, rooted in love
units.note: power by the Spirit, Christ in the heart

verses.text: I ask that out of the riches of His glory He may {{krataioo|strengthen}} you with power through His Spirit in your inner being,
so that Christ may {{katoikeo|dwell}} in your hearts through faith. Then you, being rooted and grounded in {{agape|love}},

units.label: The love that surpasses knowledge, filled with God

verses.text: will have power, together with all the saints, to comprehend the length and width and height and depth
of the love of Christ, and to know this love that surpasses knowledge, that you may be {{pleroo|filled}} with all the fullness of God.

units.label: Doxology
units.note: to Him who does immeasurably more

verses.text: Now to Him who is able to do immeasurably more than all we ask or imagine, according to His power that is at work within us,
to Him be the {{doxa|glory}} in the church and in Christ Jesus throughout all generations, forever and ever. Amen.

patria.gloss: A family, clan, or line of descent, a group deriving its name and identity from a common father. The word plays on the Father, pater, in verse 14: every patria in heaven and on earth takes its very name from Him. God is not merely one father among many but the source of all fatherhood and family, the one from whom every household, angelic and human, derives its being and its name.

krataioo.gloss: To strengthen, to make strong or mighty, to empower. Paul asks that God would krataioo the believers with power in the inner being, the hidden self, by His Spirit. This is not strength for outward feats but inward might: the deep fortifying of the soul so that Christ may make His home there and the vast love of Christ may be grasped and borne.

katoikeo.gloss: To dwell, to settle down and take up permanent residence, as opposed to a passing stay. Paul prays that Christ may katoikeo in their hearts through faith, not visit as a guest but live at home there, at the very centre of the person. It echoes the temple imagery of 2:22: the God who dwells among His people also dwells within each heart where Christ is welcomed by faith.

agape.gloss: Love, specifically the self-giving, sacrificial love that God is and that God pours out, supremely shown in Christ. Paul prays that believers, rooted and grounded in this love like a tree planted deep and a building set on firm foundation, would come to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge. It is at once the soil we grow from and the immeasurable reality we are called to comprehend.

pleroo.gloss: To fill, to make full, to bring to completeness. The prayer climbs to its height here: that believers may be filled with all the fullness of God, saturated with His own life and love and character. It does not mean becoming God, but being flooded with the moral and relational fullness of God, the very fullness that dwells in Christ, 1:23 and Colossians 2:9, now filling His people.

doxa.gloss: Glory, weight, splendour, the radiant worth and honour that belong to God. The doxology ascribes doxa to God in the church and in Christ Jesus throughout all generations. The same glorious riches from which He strengthens His people, verse 16, return to Him as praise, and remarkably the church, the reconciled temple, becomes a place where His glory is displayed and celebrated forever.

basic.context: Paul now finishes the prayer he began in verse 1. He kneels before the Father, the source of every family in heaven and on earth, and asks four great things: that God would strengthen these believers by His Spirit in their inmost being, that Christ would settle down and live in their hearts by faith, that, rooted in love, they would grasp with all the saints the vast love of Christ that is beyond knowing, and that they would be filled with all the fullness of God. He ends with a burst of praise to the God who can do far more than we ask or imagine.
basic.hermeneutics: The prayer resumes the broken sentence of verse 1, marked by the opening ellipsis. It is a single flowing petition built on the word so that: God strengthens us so that Christ dwells in us, so that we comprehend His love, so that we are filled with God's fullness. Each request leads to the next and climbs to the fullness of God, then overflows into the doxology of verses 20 and 21, the seam that closes the first half of the letter.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul kneels before the Father from whom every patria, family, is named, a play on the word Father. He asks God to krataioo, strengthen, them inwardly by the Spirit, so that Christ may katoikeo, take up permanent residence, in their hearts. Rooted in agape, love, they are to know the love of Christ and be pleroo, filled, with all the fullness of God. The doxology renders all doxa, glory, to Him in the church and in Christ Jesus forever.
basic.typology: The temple where God dwelt is fulfilled twice over here: Christ dwells within the believer's heart, and the church is filled with the fullness of God, the true sanctuary of His presence. The glory that once filled the tabernacle and temple now fills a people, and returns to God as praise from the church throughout all generations.
basic.christ: Christ stands at the centre of the prayer: He dwells in the heart by faith, His love is the immeasurable reality believers are to know, and the doxology ascribes glory to God in Christ Jesus forever. The fullness of God that fills His people is the same fullness that dwells in Christ. To be strengthened inwardly is, at heart, to have Christ make His home within.
basic.god: God is the Father from whom all fatherhood and family derive their name, rich in glory, generous in strength, and able to do immeasurably more than we ask or imagine. He is the God who fills His people with His own fullness and works His power within them. The whole prayer rests on His abundance: it is out of the riches of His glory that He gives, and to Him all glory returns.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 2 says the whole fullness of deity dwells in Christ, and you have been filled in Him. Ephesians 1 speaks of the church as the fullness of Him who fills all in all. John 14 promises that the Father and the Son will make their home with those who love Christ. Romans 8 speaks of the Spirit strengthening believers within.
basic.oneStory: The story that ran from a garden where God walked with man, through a tabernacle and temple filled with His glory, reaches a new depth here: God now dwells within His people by the Spirit and Christ makes His home in the heart. The presence once fenced behind a veil now fills a reconciled people who are being filled with all the fullness of God, and the whole movement overflows into glory given to Him forever.

context.literary: Verses 14 to 21 complete the sentence begun in verse 1 and interrupted by the digression of verses 2 to 13; the opening ellipsis signals the resumption. The prayer is a single ascending petition, each clause introduced by so that or built on the last, rising to being filled with all the fullness of God. The doxology of verses 20 to 21 closes the letter's first, largely doctrinal half, before the practical exhortations of chapters four to six.
context.historical: Kneeling was not the usual Jewish posture for prayer, which was typically standing; Paul's bowing of the knees signals unusual earnestness and reverence before the Father. Writing to believers in Ephesus, a city famed for the temple of Artemis and for magical arts prizing hidden power, Paul prays for a truer power, God strengthening the inner being by His Spirit, and a truer fullness, being filled with God Himself.
context.canonical: The prayer gathers the letter's great themes, the riches of God's glory, the Spirit, the indwelling Christ, love, and fullness, into a single petition, and its language of fullness ties back to 1:23 and forward to the mature body of 4:13. The doxology stands among the New Testament's fullest ascriptions of praise, alongside Romans 11:33-36 and Jude 24-25.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To pray for the church what its new identity requires: inward strength, the indwelling of Christ, the grasping of His love, and the filling with God's fullness, so that the glorious truths of chapters one to three are not merely known but experienced, and to close that section in worship of the God who does immeasurably more.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a prayer, so it functions less as command than as model and petition. Yet it teaches what believers are to long for and shows that such deep experience of God is His will and His gift. The abiding pattern is to seek from God, out of His riches, the inner strengthening, the indwelling Christ, the knowledge of His love, and the fullness that we cannot manufacture for ourselves.

debates.question: What does it mean to be filled with all the fullness of God?

views.label: Flooded with the moral and relational fullness of God's life and love
views.case: The prayer moves from knowing the love of Christ to being filled with God's fullness, so the sense is that believers are saturated with God's own character, love, holiness, and presence, brought to maturity as the fullness of Christ fills them, echoing 1:23 and 4:13. It is transformation into His likeness, not a change of nature into deity.

views.label: A share in the divine nature by union with Christ
views.case: Others stress that the fullness of deity dwells in Christ bodily, Colossians 2:9, and that believers are filled in Him, so being filled with God's fullness is participation in the divine life through union with Christ, while remaining creatures.

debates.bearing: Both readings guard the same boundary: believers are filled with God's own fullness and made like Him, indwelt by His Spirit and Christ, yet they do not become deity; the creature is flooded with God, not fused into God.

study.originalLanguages: Paul bows before the Father from whom every patria, family or clan, derives its name, a deliberate echo of pater, Father: God is the fountain of all fatherhood. He prays that God would krataioo, strengthen with might, the inner being by the Spirit, so that Christ may katoikeo, dwell as a permanent resident, in the heart through faith. Rooted and grounded in agape, the self-giving love supremely seen in Christ, believers are to know that very love and be pleroo, filled, with all the fullness of God. The prayer overflows into a doxology ascribing doxa, glory, to God in the church and in Christ Jesus throughout all generations.

typology.fulfillment: The glory that filled the tabernacle and temple now fills a people, as Christ dwells in the heart and the church is filled with all the fullness of God.
The promise that God would dwell in the midst of Israel is realised inwardly, Christ taking up residence in the believer's heart by the Spirit.
Every family in heaven and on earth is named from the Father, who gathers a reconciled family into His own household in Christ.

study.christ: Christ is the heart of the prayer. Paul asks that Christ would dwell, take up permanent residence, in the believer's heart through faith. The love that believers are to comprehend with all the saints is the love of Christ, so vast in its length, width, height, and depth that it surpasses knowledge, and yet is to be known. The fullness of God with which they are to be filled is the fullness that dwells in Christ. And the closing doxology gives glory to God in the church and in Christ Jesus forever, so that Christ is both the one who indwells and the one in whom God is praised.
study.god: God is the Father from whom every family in heaven and on earth is named, the source of all fatherhood and belonging. He is rich in glory and generous, strengthening His people out of that glory and filling them with His own fullness. He is the God whose power is already at work within believers and who is able to do immeasurably more than all we ask or imagine. The prayer rests wholly on His abundance, and the doxology returns all glory to Him, celebrated in the church and in Christ Jesus throughout all generations.

crossReferences.note: The church as the fullness of Him who fills all in all, the same fullness language that crowns this prayer.
The whole fullness of deity dwells in Christ, and you have been filled in Him, illuminating being filled with the fullness of God.
The Father and the Son will come and make their home with those who love Christ, parallel to Christ dwelling in the heart.
A great doxology to the God whose riches and wisdom are unsearchable, kin to the praise of verses 20 to 21.

study.oneStory: The Bible traces God's desire to dwell with His people, from walking in Eden, to filling the tabernacle and temple with glory, to the promise of a new covenant written on the heart. Here that longing reaches its inward fulfilment: Christ makes His home in the heart by faith, and the reconciled people are filled with all the fullness of God. The presence once guarded behind a veil now floods a family drawn from every nation, and the story overflows into glory given to God in the church and in Christ Jesus forever and ever.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say he bows his knees before the Father?

options.text: As an act of earnest reverence before the Father from whom every family is named.
options.note: Kneeling signals unusual earnestness; God is the source of all fatherhood and family.

options.text: Because kneeling was the only permitted posture for prayer.
options.note: The usual posture was standing; kneeling here marks special earnestness.

options.text: To draw attention to his own piety before others.
options.note: The focus is on the Father and the church's need, not on Paul's display.

options.text: Because the Father is only one family head among many equals.
options.note: Every family in heaven and on earth derives its name from Him; He is the source of all.

quiz.q: What is the first thing Paul asks God to do for the believers?

options.text: To strengthen them with power through His Spirit in their inner being.
options.note: The prayer begins with inward strengthening, out of the riches of His glory.

options.text: To give them outward success and comfort in daily life.
options.note: The request is for inner might by the Spirit, not outward ease.

options.text: To remove all suffering from their lives at once.
options.note: Paul prays for strength within; he has just called his own sufferings their glory.

options.text: To make them wealthy and powerful in the eyes of the world.
options.note: The power sought is inward, by the Spirit, so Christ may dwell within.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean when he prays that Christ may dwell in their hearts?

options.text: That Christ would take up permanent residence at the centre of their lives through faith.
options.note: The word means to settle and live at home, not to pay a passing visit.

options.text: That Christ would occasionally visit them as a guest.
options.note: The word means permanent residence, not an occasional visit.

options.text: That they would simply think about Christ from time to time.
options.note: Paul asks for a deep indwelling by faith, not mere occasional reflection.

options.text: That Christ dwells only in the church building.
options.note: Christ dwells in the hearts of believers, echoing the living temple of 2:22.

quiz.q: What does Paul pray they would come to know together with all the saints?

options.text: The love of Christ in its length, width, height, and depth, which surpasses knowledge.
options.note: The immeasurable love of Christ is to be grasped in community with all the saints.

options.text: The exact date of Christ's return.
options.note: The object of the prayer is the love of Christ, not hidden timetables.

options.text: A secret knowledge reserved for a spiritual elite.
options.note: It is grasped together with all the saints, not by an elite few.

options.text: That God's love can be fully mastered by human intellect.
options.note: This love surpasses knowledge even as we are called to know it.

quiz.q: What does being filled with all the fullness of God mean?

options.text: Being saturated with God's own life, love, and character, made like Him yet not becoming deity.
options.note: Believers are flooded with the fullness that dwells in Christ, transformed into His likeness.

options.text: Becoming divine and ceasing to be creatures.
options.note: The creature is filled with God, not fused into God; we remain His creatures.

options.text: Gaining unlimited earthly power and success.
options.note: The fullness is moral and relational, God's own character and presence.

options.text: Having nothing more to do with God's Spirit.
options.note: The filling is precisely the work of God's Spirit within His people.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe God in the closing doxology?

options.text: Able to do immeasurably more than all we ask or imagine, by His power at work within us.
options.note: The doxology exalts God's boundless ability and His power already working in believers.

options.text: Able to grant only what we can clearly picture in advance.
options.note: He does immeasurably more than we ask or imagine, beyond our picturing.

options.text: Distant and uninvolved in the life of the church.
options.note: His power is at work within us, and He is glorified in the church.

options.text: Deserving of glory for a single generation only.
options.note: Glory is given to Him throughout all generations, forever and ever.

questions: Paul prays first for inner strength, not outward ease. How would your own prayers change if you asked God most of all to strengthen your inner being by His Spirit?
Christ dwelling in the heart means settling in as a permanent resident, not a guest. What parts of your life have you kept as rooms Christ is not yet welcome to live in?
The love of Christ surpasses knowledge, yet Paul prays we would know it with all the saints. Why do you think grasping this love is something we do together rather than alone?
Paul asks that we be filled with all the fullness of God. What would it look like, this week, to live as someone being flooded with God's own life and love?
The doxology says God can do immeasurably more than we ask or imagine. Where have you been praying too small, and how does this truth enlarge your expectation of God?

translationNotes.note: The verse opens with an ellipsis in the BSB because Paul resumes here the sentence he began in 3:1 and interrupted with the digression of verses 2 to 13; the ellipsis is not a dash.
Family renders patria, which plays on pater, Father: every family or clan derives its name from the Father, who is the source of all fatherhood in heaven and on earth.

The love of Christ that surpasses knowledge yet is to be known holds together a real, growing grasp of that love with its inexhaustible greatness; the goal is to be filled with all the fullness of God, transformed into His likeness rather than turned into deity.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 3:14-21 reproduced as supplied, including the ellipsis at the start of verse 14. No em or en dashes used.
